Title: Dynamic metabolic responses of brown planthoppers towards susceptible and resistant rice plants

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Candidate target for brown planthopper control because resistant-rice feeding consistently elevates TPS expression and trehalose accumulation, suggesting TPS supports adaptation and energy supply on resistant hosts. Combining host resistance such as Bph15 with RNAi or chemical suppression of TPS could weaken insect compensation. Caution: this is an insect-control target, not a crop quality engineering target.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes formation of trehalose-6-phosphate from glucose-derived substrates in the trehalose biosynthetic pathway.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Higher TPS expression in BPH feeding on resistant rice is associated with increased trehalose accumulation, supporting adaptation to resistant plants.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: qRT-PCR of TPS transcript across feeding time points combined with NMR metabolite profiling showing trehalose and glucose changes.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly forms the trehalose precursor leading to target product.
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Feeding on Bph15-carrying resistant rice versus susceptible TN1 rice for 12, 24, 48, and 96 h.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Transcript level was significantly higher in nymphs feeding on resistant rice than on susceptible rice at all measured time points.
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Metabolomics (1H NMR; GC-FID/MS for fatty acids); targeted qRT-PCR; no public accession reported.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Trehalose is described as the main blood sugar and main energy source for hopping in insects.
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Trehalose synthesis branch of carbohydrate metabolism
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Supports insect adaptation to resistant rice; likely beneficial to pest survival rather than host plant performance.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. TPS transcript was significantly up-regulated in BPH nymphs feeding on resistant rice at all time points. 2. Trehalose levels became higher, especially at later feeding stages, while glucose tended to be reduced. 3. The study links TPS activation to energetic adaptation and increased hopping on resistant hosts.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Major insect blood sugar and energy source for locomotion/hopping.

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential pest-control target because elevated PFK expression suggests glycolytic reinforcement helps BPH adapt to resistant rice. Can be considered for insect-directed RNAi in combination with resistant rice. Caution: evidence is transcript-metabolite association, not direct enzyme manipulation.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Phosphorylates fructose-6-phosphate to fructose-1,6-bisphosphate in glycolysis.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Higher PFK expression in BPH on resistant rice is associated with enhanced glycolytic support for downstream amino acid, fatty acid and energy metabolism during adaptation.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: qRT-PCR across time points plus metabolite profiling of glycolysis/TCA-related metabolites.
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Feeding on resistant NIL-Bph15 rice versus susceptible TN1 for 12-96 h.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Significantly higher transcript abundance in nymphs feeding on resistant rice at all time points.
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Metabolomics (1H NMR; GC-FID/MS); targeted qRT-PCR; no accession reported.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Authors explicitly describe PFK as one of two key glycolytic enzymes.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Likely promotes pest energy homeostasis on resistant plants.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. PFK transcript was significantly elevated in BPH feeding on resistant rice throughout the assay. 2. Increased PFK expression paralleled recovery/increase of amino acids, fatty acids and TCA intermediates at later stages. 3. The study interprets PFK up-regulation as metabolic adaptation to resistant-host stress.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Supports insect energy metabolism indirectly through glycolytic precursor supply.

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential auxiliary pest-control target because enhanced PGK expression indicates reinforced glycolytic flux during adaptation to resistant rice. Best deployed with other targets rather than alone.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Transfers phosphate in the conversion of 1,3-bisphosphoglycerate to 3-phosphoglycerate, generating ATP in glycolysis.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Higher PGK expression in BPH on resistant rice is associated with strengthened energy generation and precursor supply for adaptive metabolism.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: qRT-PCR and metabolite profiling of central carbon metabolism.
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Feeding on resistant NIL-Bph15 rice versus susceptible TN1 for 12-96 h.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Significantly higher in BPH feeding on resistant rice at all measured time points.
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Metabolomics (1H NMR; GC-FID/MS); targeted qRT-PCR; no accession reported.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Grouped with PFK by authors as key glycolytic enzymes linked to ATP/NADH generation.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Likely improves pest resilience on resistant plants.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key enzyme but not established as rate-limiting in this paper
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. PGK transcript increased significantly in resistant-rice-fed BPH at all time points. 2. Up-regulation coincided with later recovery of central metabolites and fatty acids. 3. Authors interpret PGK induction as part of glycolytic adaptation to adverse feeding conditions.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Provides ATP for energy-demanding adaptation and locomotion.

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Strong pest-management candidate because FAS up-regulation tracks recovery and accumulation of multiple fatty acids in BPH on resistant rice. Combining host resistance with FAS silencing/inhibition may reduce adaptation and lipid storage.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes de novo synthesis/elongation of fatty acid chains from acetyl/malonyl units to produce long-chain fatty acids.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Higher FAS expression in BPH feeding on resistant rice is associated with increased fatty acid accumulation at later feeding stages, indicating adaptive lipid storage.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: qRT-PCR of FAS plus GC-FID/MS fatty acid profiling over time.
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Feeding on resistant NIL-Bph15 rice versus susceptible TN1 for 12, 24, 48, and 96 h.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Expression pattern similar to TPS, PFK and PGK; higher in BPH feeding on resistant rice across time points.
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Metabolomics (1H NMR), fatty acid profiling (GC-FID/MS), targeted qRT-PCR; no accession reported.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Later-stage increases involved major BPH fatty acids including C16:0, C18:0, C18:1 and others.
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: De novo long-chain fatty acid synthesis
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Enhances pest energy storage and adaptation to resistant hosts.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. FAS transcript showed a trend similar to TPS, PFK and PGK and was higher in resistant-rice-fed BPH. 2. Early feeding on resistant plants lowered most fatty acids, but later feeding increased many fatty acids. 3. The study links FAS induction to adaptive lipid synthesis/storage under resistant-host stress.

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Promising pest-control target because GS up-regulation is associated with recovery of amino acid metabolism on resistant rice and may cooperate with symbiont-supported nutrition. Could be stacked with host resistance and symbiont-targeting strategies.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Converts glutamate and ammonium to glutamine using ATP.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Higher GS expression in BPH on resistant rice is associated with recovery/increase of glutamine and broader amino acid metabolism during adaptation.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: qRT-PCR of GS together with NMR-based amino acid quantification.
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Feeding on resistant NIL-Bph15 rice versus susceptible TN1 for 12-96 h.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Higher in BPH feeding on resistant rice; followed same pattern as TPS.
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Metabolomics (1H NMR); targeted qRT-PCR; no accession reported.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Authors note glutamine supplies nitrogen for amino acid, pyrimidine and purine biosynthesis and is important for insect fecundity.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Likely promotes pest nutrition, fecundity and resilience.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. GS transcript was elevated in BPH feeding on resistant rice. 2. Increased GS expression paralleled later recovery of amino acid pools including glutamine. 3. The paper suggests GS contributes to adaptive nitrogen metabolism under resistant-host challenge.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Nitrogen donor for amino acid, purine and pyrimidine biosynthesis.

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Lower-priority pest-control target based on this study because no significant differential expression was observed between resistant- and susceptible-rice feeding.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes glutamate formation from glutamine and a 2-oxoacid in the glutamate synthase reaction.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: No significant expression difference was observed, so contribution to resistant-rice-associated metabolic remodeling was not supported here.
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Feeding on resistant NIL-Bph15 rice versus susceptible TN1 for 12-96 h.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: No significant difference between BPH feeding on resistant and susceptible rice.
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Targeted qRT-PCR; paired with metabolomics context; no accession reported.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The text uses GUS for glutamate synthase; no significant differences were observed.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. GUS was included among nine tested metabolic genes. 2. Unlike GS and AS, GUS showed no significant difference between treatments. 3. Therefore the study does not support a major role of differential GUS expression in adaptation to resistant rice.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Central amino donor/acceptor in nitrogen metabolism.

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential pest-control target because AS induction accompanies adaptive nitrogen storage/transport metabolism on resistant rice. Could be combined with GS-targeting approaches.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Converts aspartate to asparagine using an amide donor, supported by glutamine-dependent asparagine synthase activity.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Higher AS expression in BPH on resistant rice is associated with adaptive amino acid biosynthesis, including asparagine-related nitrogen metabolism.
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Feeding on resistant NIL-Bph15 rice versus susceptible TN1 for 12-96 h.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Expression had behavior similar to TPS, PFK, PGK, FAS and GS; higher in BPH feeding on resistant rice.
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Metabolomics (1H NMR); targeted qRT-PCR; no accession reported.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Authors specifically interpret AS together with GS as supporting amino acid compensation/adaptation, potentially with contributions from yeast-like symbionts.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Likely promotes pest adaptation to resistant plants.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. AS transcript was higher in resistant-rice-fed BPH. 2. This increase paralleled recovery of amino acid pools after the early nutrient-deficiency phase. 3. The study suggests AS contributes to adaptation of BPH nitrogen metabolism on resistant hosts.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Nitrogen transport/storage and amino acid biosynthesis.

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful insect-control target because AK is the sole phosphagen kinase in insect energy metabolism and is induced after adaptation begins on resistant rice. Could be combined with TPS inhibition or resistant rice to disrupt hopping-associated energy demand.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes reversible phosphorylation between arginine/phosphoarginine and ATP/ADP for cellular energy buffering.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: AK was initially down-regulated at 12 h on resistant rice, then up-regulated from 24 h onward, consistent with increased demand for ATP buffering during adaptive hopping and energy metabolism.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: qRT-PCR with interpretation alongside trehalose/glucose metabolite changes.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Supports energy supply rather than direct metabolite synthesis
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Feeding on resistant NIL-Bph15 rice versus susceptible TN1 for 12-96 h.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Initially decreased at 12 h on resistant rice, then increased from 24 h onwards.
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Targeted qRT-PCR; metabolomics context; no accession reported.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Text also states AK is the sole kinase for arginine phosphorylation in insects. Discussion phrase 'arginine synthase' appears to refer to AK, but Figure 5 labels AK as arginine kinase.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Supports pest locomotion and adaptation to resistant hosts.
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: Phosphoarginine or ATP depending on direction
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key enzyme in insect phosphagen metabolism
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. AK expression was lower at 12 h but higher from 24 h onward in resistant-rice-fed BPH. 2. The dynamic pattern matches early nutrient stress followed by energetic adaptation. 3. Authors relate AK induction to increased ATP/trehalose demand for hopping on resistant plants.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Rapid ATP buffering for muscle activity and energy homeostasis.

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: High-value pest-control target because CHS is consistently implicated in survival-related structural remodeling on resistant rice. Combining resistant rice with CHS knockdown or chitin synthesis inhibitors may impair exoskeleton/peritrophic membrane formation and reduce pest adaptation.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: CHS expression increased significantly after 24 h and 48 h on resistant rice, indicating enhanced chitin biosynthesis associated with adaptation to resistant-host stress.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: qRT-PCR of CHS with metabolite profiling showing lower glucose and higher UDP-glucose/trehalose context; functional interpretation from pathway biology.
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Feeding on resistant NIL-Bph15 rice versus susceptible TN1 for 12-96 h.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Significantly increased after 24 h and 48 h feeding on resistant rice; by 96 h expression became similar between treatments.
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Targeted qRT-PCR with metabolomics context; no accession reported.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Summary states BPH feeding on resistant plants showed significant up-regulation of chitin synthase throughout 96 h, but Results text specifies significant increase at 24 h and 48 h and no difference by 96 h; this inconsistency is noted.
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Structural chitin formation for cuticle, peritrophic membrane and tracheae
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Likely aids pest survival and stress protection on resistant plants.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CHS transcript increased significantly in BPH on resistant rice after 24 h and 48 h. 2. The authors link this to elevated demand for chitin in cuticle, gut lining and tracheae under stress. 3. CHS induction helps explain increased need for glucose- and glutamine-derived chitin precursor synthesis during adaptation.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Insect exoskeleton, peritrophic membrane, foregut/hindgut and tracheal linings
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Structural barrier for development and protection from biotic and abiotic stresses.
